data.title: EUSA: A Large-Cap ETF That Makes Sense Right Now
data.description:
I am a big fan of ETFs that do things a bit differently, and, right now, equal weighting rather than market-cap weighting seems to makes sense. I initiated coverage of a large fund, Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP), at the end of November with a Hold, calling it unique and compelling. It has rallied since then by 6.4% including the dividend, which is above the 0.1% total return for State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

This week, I found another equal weighted fund, the iShares MSCI USA Equal Weighted ETF (EUSA), that has basically tracked RSP for a long time but lagged it recently. I have added this ETF to my 83-stock ETF Watch List, and I am initiating it with a Hold. In this article, I discuss the ETF and assess its outlook.

What EUSA Is

Like RSP, this ETF is based on equal weights. iShares launched it more than 10 years ago in late 2015. At only $1.55 billion net assets, it is a small fund for iShares and dramatically smaller than RSP, which has a market cap of $86.8 billion. I like that EUSA is so much smaller, and I really like that its fee is lower at 0.09% vs. 0.20% for RSP. While it is much smaller, it is big enough in my view.

iShares explains the ETF on a page on its website dedicated to EUSA. It also published a Fact Sheet as of 12/31. One of the key things readers should note is that the index is very different from the S&P 500. The MSCI USA Equal Weighted Index was launched in 2011. It is rebalanced quarterly, and there are currently 544 members. As of the end of January, the top 10 were just 2.71%. Here are the top 10 in EUSA as of 2/12:

Like RSP, the sector weightings are very different from the S&P 500, which currently has about 33% in Technology, almost 13% in Financials and over 10% in Communication Services. As of 2/12, EUSA has these sector weights:

The exposure to Technology is much lower, and the Industrials exposure is substantially higher than the S&P 500, which had 9.1%. RSP has similar weightings.

iShares reports a trailing PE of 24X for EUSA, which is lower than the 29X trailing PE for IVV. The P/B ratio of 3.3X is below IVV's at 5.3X. The main difference between EUSA and ETFs that are focused on the S&P 500, like IVV and also SPY and Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) is the concentration. The top 10 names in the S&P 500 total about 37%. The median market cap is $39.5 billion, with only 5 names below $10 billion. The median of the S&P 500 is a very similar $39.3 billion.

The shares outstanding have increased by 3.2% since year-end, so perhaps the ETF is getting more popular. With only 1030 followers at Seeking Alpha, EUSA does not seem so popular at this time. RSP has 21,650 followers, substantially more. More Seeking Alpha analysts cover RSP, and its Quant Rating at 3.58 exceeds the Quant Rating for EUSA at 2.95. RSP scores slightly higher on its expenses but a bit lower on its momentum and its liquidity.

With its lower fees and its management by a larger ETF company, EUSA should be bigger than RSP. I believe that there are two factors that have led to its failure to grow in size substantially. First, being based on MSCI instead of the S&P 500 gets in the way. Second, RSP got started earlier, launching in 2003. iShares has many options for investors interested in large-caps, and it fails to include EUSA, which would be the 14th largest (9th largest U.S. focused), and this may be a factor as well. The very largest, which I include on my ETF Watch List, is iShares Core S&P 500 (IVV), and it has $749 billion in net assets with an expense ratio of just 0.03%. EUSA is listed on the iShares website as a "Large/Mid Cap", which may be a reason why its not larger.

How EUSA Has Performed

EUSA has started 2026 strongly, rising 3.9% compared to a very slight decrease in the S&P 500. RSP has gained 5.9%. Looking at the chart over the past year, EUSA has rallied sharply from its low in April and is near an all-time high:

EUSA has rallied 31.9% from the low in April and is just 1.1% below the all-time high set this past week. I see resistance forming at $108, and I see technical support potentially at $98, which is down 8.7%, and at $87, which would be a decline of 19.0%. Of course, the stock could continue to rally further, and it could also retrace more of its move from the late 2022 low of 65.35 to its recent peak. A 50% retracement would work out to about $87, which is my second support level, but a 61.8% Fibonacci retracement would drop it to about $82. A 50% retracement of the move from the low in early 2020 after the pandemic hit would drop the stock to $74, a potential drop of 31%.

As I said above, RSP and EUSA have tracked each other well over time. Here is the past 10 years along with IVV:

Despite the lower management fee, EUSA has lagged RSP. Both have greatly lagged the S&P 500 over the past three years despite the recent improvement.

EUSA Could Perform Better than the S&P 500

I have shared some technical perspectives about EUSA, and now I want to discuss some scenarios regarding its potential performance in 2026. As a large-cap ETF, its performance will be similar to the S&P 500. One of the biggest factors impacting it will be interest rates, which could continue to see falling Fed Funds. I am not expecting the Fed to cut rates further and appreciate the tough spot the U.S. is in right now with respect to potential inflation. I am extremely concerned with the massive and growing federal debt and believe that the steepening of the yield curve reflects the concerns of others.

In 2026, long-dated Treasuries have increased in yield slightly, while the 2-year is roughly unchanged and the 5-year is lower. Going back to the end of 2024, which was after a new President was elected, Fed Funds have been cut three times to a new range of 3.50-3.75% from 4.25-4.50%. The 2-year Treasury yield has declined 83 basis points to 3.51%, the 5-year has dropped 64 bps to 3.74%, the 10-year has declined 10 bps to 4.17%, and the 30-year has increased 4 bps to 4.82%. Corporate spreads are very low. The 30-year fixed-rate mortgage, according to Freddie Mac, was 6.09% on 2/12, which is down from 6.85% near the end of 2024.

My outlook for 2026 for the S&P 500 is that it won't likely have a double-digit gain for a fourth-straight year. While I don't expect stocks to keep rising, they certainly could do so. My bullish scenario would be for the S&P 500 to rally 10%, and I assign this a 25% chance. My neutral outlook is for the S&P 500 to rise 3%, and I give this scenario a 50% chance. My bearish outlook for the S&P 500 is a potential drop of 17%, and this gets a 25% weighting too. Of course, the S&P 500 could go up more than I am assessing or down more than I am assessing, and my weightings may be off. The projected return for 2026 works out to -0.25% or 0.8% including the dividends. This is worse than cash.

How EUSA performs will depend upon primarily how the stocks within the S&P 500 perform. The outperformance of the smaller companies may continue, or it may reverse. In my bullish scenario, I assume that the larger companies get back on track, and I give EUSA a potential price return of 8% (a total return of 9.6%). In the neutral case, I assume that EUSA benefits from continued outperformance of smaller companies with a total return of 6.6%. It is the bearish scenario that will help EUSA stand out compared to the S&P 500, and I am assuming a total return of -10.3%. Weighting these scenarios results in a total return of 3.1%, which is better than the S&P 500 return that I project.

Conclusion

While I am not initiating coverage with a Buy rating, I do like EUSA more than large-cap ETFs like IVV, SPY and VOO. Again, owning large-cap ETFs leaves holders highly exposed to the largest names in the S&P 500. Equal weighting with EUSA or RSP will protect against potential continued reversals in these largest names.

For those who own large-cap ETFs, EUSA may be a substitute for them, or it may be a way to commit more money to large-caps without adding to the exposure they already have to the very largest stocks. While iShares says that EUSA is large-cap and mid-cap, it looks very similar to RSP and has a very large-cap exposure.

Traders and investors who deal in individual stocks should be aware that the very largest stocks in the S&P 500 face risks and should make sure that they are assessing the current valuations at these higher prices. It's important for everyone to understand that things do change!

data.title: Style-Box Update: Value Outperforms Across All Market Caps
data.description:
This spreadsheet analysis which is typically posted to the blog shows that “value” investing is clearly outperforming growth investing.

Last Q1 ’25, during the Liberation Day correction, the same thing happened, but once the stock market bottomed in early April ’25, growth investing started to resume its former leadership.

This time - or rather in Q1 ’26 - there is no external catalyst to explain the reversal from growth to value.

When the last Style-Box Update was written on January 6th, with year-end ’25 returns and annual returns as well, value was just starting to creep up on growth returns in Q4 ’25.

The January 6th, ’26 post does suggest, with international starting to outperform in ’25, that “all this is evidence of the shifting landscape in the broader equity market performance.”

As the old saying goes, "things are a' changin," but let’s not dramatically overreact.

The S&P 500 and Walmart

This daily chart of the S&P 500 from Worden shows the key benchmark in the early stages of getting oversold, but it was more oversold last November ’25.

The S&P 500 still has not taken out the 7,000 level. Not a shock given the performance of the Mag 7 / 10 stocks, and now the outperformance of value across the style boxes.

Walmart (WMT) reports Thursday morning, February 19th, 2026, before the opening bell. The consumer staples sector has been on fire, led by Walmart and Costco (COST). Costco bottomed two days before Christmas on December 23, ’25, and has run from $843 to close at $1,018 last Friday, February 13th, ’26.

Here’s a table from Bespoke’s weekend report dated February 13, ’26, which shows the extreme overbought nature of Walmart and the whole staples sector.

A reader asked a question if I were thinking of selling any Walmart before earnings, and in fact, some Walmart was sold prior to this coming Thursday’s earnings report. It’s a guess, but there is still probably 75-80% of the original WMT position being held, and more partial positions might be trimmed this week. The addition of Walmart to the Nasdaq 100 index really brought some accumulation into the stock after that announcement.

Summary / conclusion

The style box analysis confirms the relative outperformance of value versus growth across the market caps, but the question is how long does it last, and just how much “alpha” can value generate versus growth, given its long period of underperformance.

Consumer staples stocks always sport “growth valuations” given the consistency and dependability of their revenue, operating income and EPS results. Coke (KO) missed when they reported their Q4 ’25 results. The stock dropped a little following, but then ramped sharply from $72 to close at $78 last Friday, being pulled along by the staples burst of momentum. Walmart will be an important tell this week. (Earnings preview will be published.)

The mega-caps, the “growth trade” (including larger-cap banks) and the market leadership the last 4 years, was due for a break, and the continued enormous capex builds around AI have given the former market leadership pause.

Even international and emerging markets are overbought, and the dollar is oversold (long JPMorgan Int’l Value, i.e., JFEAX and JIVE), the emerging markets ex-China (EMXC) ETF, and some Japan (EWJ ETF).

A reversal trade from here will see the old leaders rally and the “new leadership” correct. That will be the litmus test for the new trade.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Clients and readers should gauge their own comfort with portfolio volatility and adjust accordingly. None of the above information may be updated, and if updated, may not be done so in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The 2 Big Tailwinds Behind Applied Digital's New Buildout
data.description:
Thesis

Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) has outlined much improved growth trajectory as of its last earnings. Their hyperscale AI campus strategy is continuing this transition from construction to revenue generation, which is exactly what we want to see. Management now projects that it will surpass $1 billion in net operating income within five years, no mean feat. Management also told us that the Polaris Forge 1 has now reached ready-for-service with 100 MW energised, so that now makes the first phase of a 400 MW campus fully leased to CoreWeave and should represent about $11 billion in prospective lease revenue over 15 years. elsewhere the Polaris Forge 2 is set to add another 200 MW under a 15-year, $5 billion lease with an investment-grade hyperscaler.

So if we take these together, the agreements total 600 MW and about $16 billion in contracted lease revenue. This is a pathway to providing long-duration cash flow visibility as capacity comes online through 2027. However, how they achieve this is another thing. The two best things Applied Digital now have moving forward are the knowledge they learnt from the Polaris Forge 1 campus build and their power to cost advantage as I'll explain.

2Q26 recap

As a recap on earnings, Applied Digital delivered a pretty transformational 2Q26, with the stock jumping about 8% on the day of earnings. The boost was mainly driven by the ramp-up of their hyperscale AI infrastructure strategy, which we’ll get to. Revenue for the company managed to surge to $126.6 million, which is a 250% year-over-year increase, with Polaris Forge 1 reaching Ready-for-Service and also beginning to generate both tenant fit-out fees and initial lease revenue from CoreWeave. From this growth, about $85 million came from the newly operational HPC Hosting business, which is rather impressive. It shows us the company is shifting away from legacy crypto-centric hosting toward those long-term, contracted AI and HPC workloads. GAAP net loss attributable to common shareholders remained quite sizable at $31.2 million, as a reminder that they’re still in an early-stage cost structure of large-scale infrastructure deployment. Adjusted EBITDA rose pretty sharply to $20.2 million, a figure more than triple that of a year ago. So clearly, the core operating platform is moving toward profitability with capacity gradually coming online. Importantly, the overall mix of revenues is changing for them, with stable lease income now beginning to replace rather volatile hosting revenue. I think this should structurally improve earnings quality and visibility over time.

As for the balance sheet, things are also looking strong. Applied Digital managed to strengthen its financial position considerably with some aggressive capital formation aligned with long-duration contracts. Total assets expanded, now up to $5.2 billion, a figure supported by about $2.0 billion in property and equipment tied to its AI factory buildouts. As for cash, cash and restricted cash rose to roughly $2.3 billion. Elsewhere, their long-term debt increased quite substantially to $2.6 billion after the $2.35 billion senior secured note issuance and preferred equity draws from Macquarie, if you remember. We have to keep in mind that this leverage is paired with contracted hyperscaler revenue totalling roughly $16 billion over lease terms, so it should create a strong asset-liability match going forward. Liquidity also remains robust, giving the company a decent runway to complete construction and stabilise cash flows before major maturities in 2030. Operationally speaking, as you know, the company is executing a very capital-intensive but important pivot. They have hyperscaler leases at Polaris Forge 1 and 2, locking in long-term NOI potential whilst trying to reduce dependence on cyclical crypto markets. So overall, the quarter is showing us that transition of Applied Digital from a speculative data-centre operator into more of an emerging hyperscale infrastructure landlord for AI workloads, which is what we want. Yes, they’re still loss-making on a GAAP basis due to the hefty financing and stock-based compensation, but we’re seeing it increasingly get supported by contracted revenue and scalable margins.

Capacity ramp-up

Just recently, Applied Digital announced groundbreaking at Delta Forge 1. I think this is a very positive sign coming after earnings and puts them on the right path for their hyperscale AI Factory model, especially in the context of moving from regional success into national deployment. It's worth noting that the 430-MW campus isn’t just being engineered as a data centre site, but as a power-to-compute conversion engine. The goal here is to explicitly address what management identifies as the new bottleneck in AI infrastructure. This is the ability to transform raw utility power into live, high-density AI capacity at scale, but also on schedule to capitalise. Other operators in the space, such as Iren Limited (IREN), are also highlighting this bottleneck and going after it pretty aggressively. So in designing the campus to support about 300 MW of critical IT load, but across two initial 150-MW facilities, Applied Digital is setting up the Delta Forge 1 more as a modular, repeatable asset rather than a bespoke project.

We’re clearly seeing them leverage lessons learned from Polaris Forge in North Dakota in terms of standardising construction, cooling integration, and operational workflows. What I mean by that is that they’re very deliberately operationalising what Polaris Forge taught them about how AI factories actually fail or succeed at scale. If you remember, at Polaris Forge, Applied Digital had to solve three pretty hard problems at once. First, how to deliver massive power reliably? Second, how to remove extreme heat from dense GPU clusters? And finally, how to operate the facility without constant bespoke engineering fixes? Now, these lessons are being baked into a somewhat of a standardised AI Factory template for the company. For example, rather than redesigning electrical distribution for each campus, they can now just replicate the already proven high-voltage substation layouts and redundant bus architectures that they know already managed to handle 100 MW blocks at Polaris Forge 1. I think this should reduce any commissioning risk and also shorten construction timelines.

On the cooling side of things, Polaris Forge forced them to integrate liquid cooling alongside traditional air systems just to support ultra-high rack densities, so Delta Forge can now deploy that hybrid cooling stack as a default design rather than just an experiment. We’re seeing pre-sized manifolds, pumping capacity, and also thermal zoning already being modelled from real-world load behaviour. Operationally speaking, Polaris Forge proved that hyperscaler fit-outs interact with base building systems, so that shows them exactly where tenant power draw spikes and where maintenance bottlenecks emerge. This also comes hand in hand with how to get staffing scaling with megawatts. So Delta Forge can use those data points to most probably have things like pre-built control rooms, automate monitoring, and structure shift coverage around those known load profiles. It somewhat puts this new build as a second-generation factory line, where construction sequencing and operating procedures can just be cloned from a live, already revenue-producing campus.

If we focus on the scale of the site, it’s over 500 acres with long-term expansion potential, so that right there signals an intent to anchor multi-decade hyperscaler relationships at the site rather than just opportunistic capacity sales. Most importantly, however, the company is advancing the project alongside discussions with a prospective investment-grade hyperscaler. So that tells us capital deployment is being also aligned with contract visibility rather than speculatively building out the site. Economically speaking, this project just deepens Applied Digital’s role as an infrastructure landlord for AI workloads. There are long-duration and utility-like assets that should support predictable cash flows once leased, whilst also giving the company in regional development via permanent employment and utility partnerships.

Power cost advantage

One of the main reasons to be bullish on Applied Digital is its power-cost advantage. It's a massive edge they have moving forward with these buildouts and one that is mainly rooted in how and where it builds, not just in who they buy electricity from at the end of the month. You see, their campuses are intentionally sited in regions like North Dakota and now the southern U.S. Here we have large blocks of utility power that can be secured at structurally much lower rates than coastal hyperscale markets. We often see this tied to long-term industrial tariffs rather than short-term wholesale exposure. So, in practical buildout terms, it means things like their substations, transmission tie-ins, and utility interconnects are being engineered at the new site from day one for 100- to 150-MW blocks. This avoids the layered, incremental upgrades we usually see, driving up the per-MW costs in dense metro areas.

As for Polaris Forge, this has allowed Applied Digital to bring about 100 MW online with a cost base aligned more to legacy industrial power rather than the kind of pricing we see for urban data centres. So the company has cheaper dollars per kilowatt-hour before they get to things like cooling and infrastructure efficiency. It's a massive advantage that I see them compounding with their cooling design in that lower ambient temperatures can reduce mechanical cooling load, whilst liquid-cooling integration cuts that parasitic power draw per rack. So as a result, you get more each megawatt purchased, actually converted into usable IT load.

In relation to their buildout economics, this should directly lower both capex with a smaller cooling plant per MW of IT, but also opex in that fewer kilowatt-hours are lost to heat rejection. It means Applied Digital can offer hyperscale customers the high-density capacity they want, but also at a lower all-in cost per GPU-hour, which makes their business very attractive for a potential hyperscaler shopping for capacity. Delta Forge also extends this logic. It has a power-first architecture, where they secure the utility envelope and then engineer the campus around it. Finally, they then layer in repeatable building modules so the power contract becomes the very foundation of the asset’s margin profile rather than another variable expense. What I’m trying to say here is that their power-cost advantage isn’t just a single cheap contract, but it’s a structural design choice they're embedding into essentially everything: site selection, electrical layout, and thermal strategy. So it turns the energy from a bottleneck into a competitive moat.

Valuation: priced for flawless execution

Applied Digital, as you would expect, is trading at a valuation with a lot of extreme growth expectations baked in rather than current earnings power. It’s no surprise that a large chunk of the market is rather bullish on players such as Applied Digital; however, the results are a valuation profile that looks rather stretched. They currently hold an enterprise value of about $11.2 billion and a trailing twelve-month revenue which is still ramping. The stock trades at about 42x EV to sales on a trailing basis and 32x EV to sales on a forward basis, which is nearly ten times the sector median. It means investors are pricing in rapid hyperscaler lease-up and long-term NOI expansion from their long-term AI Factory campuses.

Traditional earnings multiples, such as price to earnings, are, of course, not yet meaningful since they hold continued GAAP losses, so the result is that revenue-based valuation is the most relevant lens here. On a balance sheet basis, the stock trades at about 6.8x price-to-book, which is also well above the sector average, again showing that much of its market value is tied to future monetisation of capital-intensive infrastructure rather than existing profitability.

So in short, here the big risk for investors who want to get into Applied Digital stock is buying in at the right time at values they’re comfortable with. Currently, you can pick shares up in the mid-$35 range, but this price has been rather volatile, with shares dropping close to $26 just over a week ago. However, in the longer run, I do see a lot more upside to current levels, even though right now the stock is valued as a high-conviction AI infrastructure growth platform, so execution risk is high, but so is embedded upside if contracted hyperscale capacity converts into stable cash flow.

Looking ahead

Moving forward, Applied Digital seems pretty well set up to continue with this same transition from a capital-intensive build phase into a monetisation and scaling phase. They have 600 MW of contracted capacity set to be delivered through to 2027. With the addition of long-dated hyperscale leases tipped for multi-billion-dollar revenue visibility and a very modular, repeatable campus model that they proved at Polaris Forge. So the company’s focus will now shift toward executing additional campuses in similarly advantaged power markets. I expect construction timelines and unit economics to tighten moving forward. The key swing factors I see will be the speed of delivery of that capacity and also how low-cost they can get power for future sites. They've shown us disciplined capital structuring, and now management needs to execute. The big risk is in that execution, in that it now needs to be next to flawless to justify this premium on the stock.

data.title: Northrop Grumman: Underwhelming Growth Today, F/A-XX Win Could Change The Story
data.description:
Northrop Grumman (NYSE:NOC) reported its fourth quarter earnings in January beating analyst estimates on top and bottom line. Since my last report, the stock prices of the U.S. defense contractor advanced 12.4% meeting and exceeding my price target while the S&P 500 lost 1.5% showing the strength of aerospace and defense investments. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q4 2025 earnings, the outlook for 2026 and I updated my price target as I continue to the believe the stock remains a buy.

Northrop Grumman Sees Double Digit Sales Growth

In the fourth quarter, sales increased 10% to $11.7 billion compared to $10.7 billion a year ago. The performance beat analyst estimates by $80 million. Operating income grew 10% to $1.19 billion indicating stable margins.

At Aeronautics, sales increased 18% to $3.9 billion. This was driving by higher deliveries for the F-35, the ramp up of the next-generation E-130J TACAMO (Take Charge and Move Out) program on which Northrop Grumman is the prime contractor and higher B-21 volumes. The higher volumes on the B-21 program were driven by liquidation of inventory as awards for the Low-Rate Initial Production Lot 3 and advanced procurement funding for Lot 5 allows inventory to be recognized as cost of sales with an associated revenue being recognized. Operating profit increased 20% to $370 million driven by higher volume flowing through the systems in the fourth quarter allowing for operating income growth to outpace sale growth.

Defense Systems sales increased 7% or 12% organically to $2.15 billion driven by higher rocket motor productions for the GMLRS program, higher sales for the Integrated Battle Command Systems and ramp up of the Sentinel program. Defense Systems operating profit decreased 2% to $205 million driven by lower favorable catch-up adjustments and programs such as the Sentinel affected margins. The program is currently in the early phase and in the early phase typically programs bring in a lower margin that dilute overall margins.

Mission System sales rose 10% to $3.45 billion driven by production volumes, international radars and F-35 content driving a 9% increase in operating profits to $510 million. We do note that margins were modestly lower, but that is not concerning. Looking at the mix, we see production volumes as a driver which typically carry lower margins than sustainment sales while international radars is a competitive space and F-35 sales happen on a fixed margin basis.

Space Systems sales grew 5% to $2.86 billion driven by better higher production of solid rocket motors for Amazon’s Project Leo and higher classified sales. Operating profits increased 17% to $322 million driven by favorable cost adjustments, contract mix improving and volumes driving better fixed cost absorption.

Northrop Grumman Guidance Is Not Impressive

For 2026, the company is expecting between $43.5 billion and $44 billion in sales compared to nearly $42 billion in sales in 2025. Segment operating income has been guided at $4.85-$5 billion indicating segment margins of around 11%. EPS has been guided at $27.4-$27.90 compared to $26.34 a year ago. Free cash flow is guided between $3.1 billion and $3.5 billion compared to $3.3 billion in 2025. So, it is not necessarily an impressive free cash flow guidance.

Aeronautics revenues are guided at mid-$13 billion with margins of low to mid 9% while Defense Systems is guided at mid-to-high $8 billion with 10% margins, high $12 billion for mission systems with high 14% margins and Space Systems sales at $11 billion with 11% margins.

The overall guidance for 2026 is not one that impresses.

Northrop Grumman Is Not The Most Attractive Defense Stock But Still A Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Northrop Grumman is not a company with the most impressive growth numbers. For the years ahead, I expect 5.3% compounded annual growth on sales, 5.6% on EBITDA and 3.6% on free cash flow. Especially the free cash flow growth is somewhat soft and it shows that high demand for defense equipment is not necessarily drive high growth in the near-term but it drives more consistent growth. I am, however, maintaining my buy rating as Northrop Grumman should be able to increase share repurchases while maintaining a stable net debt leverage. My price target for the stock is $745.16 driving 6% upside in the base case scenario and 16% in a more upbeat scenario.

We see that EBITDA is set to grow in the years ahead driven by sales growth and modest margin expansion. That should drive some free cash flow growth but that is the lease appealing metric I would say. Capital intensity is expected to remain moderate with a dividend yield or yield on cost that is not particularly attractive. It is just a “nice to have”. I have modelled in a stable net-debt leverage with cash that is freed up from maintaining a stable leverage being directed towards share repurchases.

Conclusion: Northrop Grumman Appeal Comes From Shareholder Repurchases

The growth of Northrop Grumman is not necessarily impressive and does not drive a lot of upside. However, I do believe that combined with shareholder returns the return should be more attractive. For the moment I am maintaining my buy rating even though Northrop Grumman does not satisfy the criterion of 10% annual upside that I usually apply. I believe that besides the defense supercycle, a win for the F/A-XX could be a real catalyt for Northrop Grumman's valuation.

data.title: Rates Spark: Eurozone Pushing For Global Euro, But Not Overnight
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist and Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

Strengthening the euro’s role can help EGB demand

The global role of the euro is back in focus, but mostly as a long-term play - yet, the positive momentum could already benefit European debt. Over the weekend, the European Central Bank announced an expansion of its repo lines to the global central bank community, which clearly signals the ECB’s push to strengthen the euro’s position. In theory, this could boost the demand for eurozone government bonds (EGBs), but we are aware that much more needs to be done to challenge the dollar’s dominance.

A key reason for foreign central banks to hold US Treasury bonds as opposed to EGBs is linked to trade activity, something that won’t change overnight. FX reserves mostly reflect the composition of a country’s trade, and dollars are still simply the most widely used trade currency. US President Donald Trump’s protectionist measures could reduce the role of the dollar in trade, but this is not something that will happen overnight - rather as more of a slow trend.

One could also have a more positive take and argue that the eurozone’s conscious efforts to boost the euro’s role can already translate into higher demand for EGBs in the near term. We see that EGB spreads are hitting new lows, suggesting healthy demand overall. In a deep dive previously, we identified foreign buyers as playing an important role in absorbing the record amount of government supply hitting markets in 2026.

Many other major bond markets are facing their own challenges (think of the US and Japan), and the eurozone is therefore growing more attractive as a safe haven in a relative sense. Having said that, the eurozone will first need to figure out whether the role of a global reserve currency would be fully supported internally. On Monday, for instance, the French government called for an assessment of the negative consequences of a stronger euro.

Tuesday's events and market views

After this morning's UK jobs data showing lower-than-expected wage growth, the main focus will be on Germany’s release of the ZEW survey. The consensus here is looking for some improvement on both the expectations (to another high since 2021) and current conditions components (still in deeply negative territory). The improvement could foreshadow the PMIs at the end of the week, where the expectation for improvement is more muted. Still, being in expansionary territory and in terms of direction, the outcomes would set a counterpoint to the current bullish trend in rates.

The US returns from a holiday, but the data front remains relatively quiet for now, with only the weekly ADP employment change, Empire Manufacturing and NAHB housing index of note. The Federal Reserve's Michael Barr and Mary Daly speak on AI in the economy and the labour market, a topic that could attract more attention given that the incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh is a proponent of the theory that the new technology will deliver significant productivity gains, allowing for lower rates all else equal.

Primary markets will see Finland auctioning 10y and 15y bonds for €1.5bn and the UK selling 2y and 6y gilts for a total of £1.25bn. Croatia has mandated banks for a new 10y benchmark bond.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Bumble: I See Only One Way Out
data.description:
Bumble (BMBL) is a stock that has fallen 66% in one year and has fallen more than 96% since its IPO. Its valuation has also been greatly compressed, and now the company is trading as a virtually bankrupt business. It is impressive, with multiples such as 2x cash flow and 3x EBITDA, which means that the entire business would pay for itself as long as the financials hold up for a few years.

But still, fundamentally, it does not seem like a buy to me. The moats are deteriorating, the trends are not favorable, and the financials follow suit, so my prospects for this company are very poor. But since the valuation is extremely low, I think there is a way out: some M&A event.

Why Bumble's Business Isn’t Compelling

Bumble's business is like that of a social network; it relies heavily on moats such as network effects. Although it is not such an interesting moat, Meta (META) has proven that this business can work. But Bumble's case is quite different, as its apps are not that “social,” with a very narrow focus on dating. This already opens up some room to say that the network effect here is not that high, since the switching cost is low. The market is saturated with apps and features like that. Facebook's dating feature, Instagram - either for dating or friendship, which is a feature via Bumble For Friends -, or Tinder, the main competitor.

There is not as much loyalty as there is to Meta apps. When we think of WhatsApp, for Europeans and Latin Americans, the switching cost is crystal clear. There are other alternatives, but all your social circle and potential business and services are on WhatsApp. For anyone in these regions, not using WhatsApp isn't a real option. But now, look at these 2 charts from Bumble. The Paying users are declining.

To be honest, the analysis could end here. It wouldn't even be worth going too far into the financials and valuation because Bumble is a company that is unable to sustain its main KPI (users/paying users). Several factors drive this: the hype surrounding these apps having passed and not being a secular trend as expected, competition.

It is not just a question of how much revenue we can expect for next year, but also a question of BMBL’s sustainability, margin due to the loss of operating leverage, and so on.

Beyond external reasons, there were some mistakes in timing and vision, in my opinion. Bumble acquired an app aimed at Gen Z a few years ago, and in Q2 2025, this was recognized as an impairment loss of $400 million, i.e., it was too expensive an acquisition and did not bear the fruit the company had hoped for.

Talking about financials, adjusted EBITDA for Q3 was $83 million, and I thought I would see many adjustments related to stock-based compensation (SBC), but in fact, BMBL is quite healthy (at least yet). GAAP net income was $51 million, so after removing depreciation, any impairments, and (a reasonable) SBC, we have an adjusted EBITDA margin of ~33%, a good surprise in my view. Cash flow also follows these numbers, and in Q3 the company managed to generate $76 million in operating activities.

Why I Don't Think BMBL Is A Sell (For Now)

There are a few reasons why I don't think BMBL is a sell. The first is that the valuation asymmetry is too good. This could really be a cigar butt, which would be a company that no one values very highly, but still has one last gasp. Note that even with the market expecting a further 10% decline in revenue in 2026, we are talking about a forward P/S of 0.4x. It's a bizarrely low multiple. The P/E for 2026 is 3.4x, and cash flow is also in the low single digits.

What I mean here is that even though I don't have confidence in BMBL's business, the valuation is asymmetric. If anything positive happens, it could trigger a re-rating, and if it starts trading at 1x forward sales, we're already talking about an upside of more than 100%. That's not my base case scenario, but the asymmetry comes from: the worst that can happen is the company goes bankrupt, going to zero, but if something goes right, it could unlock a lot of upside.

And what I think could happen is some kind of M&A event. I don't think it's an obvious scenario, which is why I don't think BMBL stock is a buy, and I even think the case is too complex and involves too many risks. But in any case, I think an M&A is possible.

BMBL has a market cap of ~$420 million and net debt of ~$300 million ($589 million in total debt and $307 million in cash), i.e., an enterprise value of just over $700 million. Meta bought Instagram for $1 billion more than 10 years ago, and the platform had no revenue and had ~50 million users. Bumble has 3.6 million paying users alone, which means that with free users, the total users could reach some number similar to Instagram in that time.

But even so, I don't think Meta would want to buy Bumble. As much as I think the comparison with Instagram is interesting to show that Bumble's price is currently very low, Meta bought Instagram for the future, to use its expertise in growing and monetizing. So it might make more sense for Meta to continue developing new features on existing platforms than to buy and try to solve Bumble's problem, which is not a small one.

However, as I mentioned, I still think an M&A event is possible. This could happen, for example, through a private equity firm or some other player interested in expanding in this niche, since Meta is not the only company with the capacity to make such an offer. A PE firm could want to close the company and have more control to try to solve and optimize cash flow, after all, the shareholder yield here is huge and the investment would pay for itself just by maintaining the current cash flow at the same level for a few more years, and the balance sheet is healthy.

BMBL’s Q4 earnings are next week, and I think it's very unlikely that there will be any operational announcement that would be enough to change my perspective. Obviously, if the company can prove that it is on track to stabilize paying users and eventually return to growth, that would be great, as would any initiative to diversify these users and explore new trends. What could really attract attention and unlock value would be an announcement of an expected deal.

The Bottom Line

Bumble is not yet a company doomed to bankruptcy, its balance sheet is positive, and its financials are still at healthy levels, but operations are not good, and the prospects are quite challenging. What balances this out is the valuation. But since I don't like to rely on M&As and similar events to unlock upside, I prefer to stay out of BMBL stock and reinforce that this is a complex and risky case. My rating is a “cautious” hold.

data.title: BSOL: Solana At The Crossroads
data.description:
ETF managers rarely get the timing right.

The Bitwise Solana Staking ETF (BSOL) debuted last fall and practically rang the bell of its own market top. It launched on October 28 at $25.55. It now trades at $11.33, having lost more than half its value in less than four months. Ouch. . ..

At a longer glance, though, the fund has a lot to like. Its .20% expense ratio is quite modest for the innovative way it leans into a genuinely new asset via price appreciation and income. Unlike a standard spot price ETF, BSOL (market cap: $477 million) is unique in that it is a “staking” fund. While offering direct exposure to Solana, it also generates staking income (staking 100% of its assets via its partner Helius) to add to total returns. This allows investors to lean into additional income --re-invested and not distributed, presently 6.77%-- as transaction demand expands. Placed into a Roth or traditional IRA, the staking rewards incur no taxes (and avoids UBIT).

The Solana platform has long been known as the proverbial “strong horse” of the crypto field. Launched in 2020 by engineers looking for better performance, its raw metrics are impressive. It is known for its low latency—i.e. it can confirm a transaction in a mere 400 milliseconds (compared to, say, 12 seconds for Ethereal (ETHE), or a glacial 10 minutes for Bitcoin.) Besides being fast, it also offers a very low transaction cost of .01, again much cheaper and better than its primary competitor ETHE.

More impressively, the platform can process 100,000 financial transactions per second, which is more activity than all the stock markets of the world combined. This is clearly the kind of functionality a platform will need if it is going to do the future’s heavy lifting. And indeed, Solana generated over $2.8 billion in network revenue in the past year, more than any other chain.

The Down Draft

For all the talk of crypto being an alternative asset, it has performed and correlated much more like a market liquidity indicator. The past few months have been tough sledding. Unlike tech, the crypto market never recovered from the October drop, seeing no reprieve in December or January.

Of course, the liquidity cycle has always been the Great Humbler, and to fight against it as futile as Cuchulain’s strikes against the waves. A serious drawdown can make a ticker go from a momentum play to a contrarian play in the blink of an eye.

Solana-USD has plummeted from $247 in mid-September to $86 this week, with nearly 38% of that screeching slump coming in the past month. That is what market analysts typically call an “extreme fear” condition, when negative sentiment dislocates price with indiscriminate selling.

However, this wasn’t all sentiment. In late December, a class action lawsuit against digital casino app Pump.fun (“with a name like that, what could go wrong…”) appeared to expand to the Solana Foundation, a mission-critical institution of the ecosystem. The prosecution alleges RICO-like schemes and the rigging of coin launches, pointing to over 5,000 emails between Foundation and Pump.Fun engineers that supposedly relate to “rug pull” manipulation and first/best price access.

Again, it is important to register that class action attorneys typically go for the deepest pockets and the conversations between engineers might not be known or sanctioned by management, but a high-profile lawsuit is never a good look for a platform’s pre-eminent institution. Shoes often keep dropping over the course of a long legal process.

The news clearly helped to take the coin down hard in January, leaving it well below its moving averages:

The coin saw a very oversold RSI throughout most of February, with the price going as low as $69 but essentially bouncing off its 2022 consolidation support of $78 - $82.

Does this qualify as Baron Rothchild's “blood in the streets”? Yup, though there could be some additional arterial bleed-out.

Solana fell into the $10 range back in late 2022, during the liquidity collapse of the so-called UK Gilt Crisis:

It had fallen for 13 months (Nov. 2021 to Dec. 2022). It only stabilized in the low $20s in early ’23.

The tale of the tape is not destiny, but it is a record of support, resistance, and the parameters of conviction. In fact, the RSI is now as low as we saw in the depths of ’22 --a very positive indicator. And Solana is seeing an institutional-level embrace (Visa, Treasury) that was simply unthinkable in ‘21 or ’22.

However, it does suggest that we need to be sanguine as to the length of these crypto winters.

Outlook

Solana-USD is clearly at a juncture, but is it at a near-term or a long-term bounce?

I ask each you, as individual investors, to scrutinize Solana at this price and to deploy your own due diligence. There are some impressive things to like here. Solana is arguably the one blockchain platform best equipped to handle the future trends in stable coin, tokenization, and AI agency.

Solana is a blockchain platform with real “smart contract” functionality, and with developers offering a wide range of DeFi services and “decentralized apps” (dApps). As mentioned earlier, it offers exceptional speed, processing power, and distinguishing cost. It feels like an evolutionary rung higher than both Bitcoin and Ethereum and arguably renders both mute as platforms of high performance.

Exhibit A of Solana’s relevance: in December Visa (V) officially launched a stablecoin settlement for two US banking partners on the Solana blockchain. Two banks –Cross River and Lead—will be the first participants settling with the credit card juggernaut via Circle’s USCD. Nikhil Chandhok, Circle’s Chief Technology Officer called the event “a milestone for internet native money moving at the speed of software."

Visa said it chose the Solana platform for its high throughput & low fees. It will enable much faster 24/7 treasury operations. This event didn’t get a lot of press when it hit, but it is rather momentous, suggesting which “rails” Visa will be moving on for its hefty transactions in the US. As one Visa head declared: “Financial institutions are looking for faster, programmable settlement options that integrate seamlessly with their existing treasury operations.”

Visa already has stable coin settlement “pilot programs” in various countries in the Pacific, Latin America and the Middle East, and its monthly stable coin volume hit a $3.5 billion annualized run rate late last year. (It’s still just pilot programs, but because it's Visa it’s already rounding up to big money. Translation: the future’s so bright, you’ll have to wear shades.)

In addition to its in-roads via Visa, Solana could see additional demand from savers worldwide. The US may seek to use stablecoins to bolster the dollar (and treasury demand) in the face of central bank diversification by “going directly” to Developing World savers trapped in weak currencies (i.e. Egypt, Turkey, Nigeria, etc) who would prefer a digital solution to “currency in the mattress.”

Because behind every stablecoin is a T-bill, this qualifies as an interesting, forward-looking “new way” to keep treasury demand up. The Genius Act of July 2025 offers a clear regulatory framework for USD-backed stablecoins, providing them with eligible reserves. In turn the coins support the treasury market. This demand increases liquidity for government debt, with coin issuers acting as a growing buyer of short-term T bills and now holding more than $200 billion.

Tokenization (RWAs)

As a high-performance/low-cost “proof of history,” Solana provides an ideal platform for those companies pioneering the tokenization of real-world assets. It also enables 24/7 trading and fractional ownership for those stocks, bonds, and real estate that are “assigned” digital tokens on the chain.

Though I see pushback from exchanges and even governments (and even agree on the merits of “jurisdictional siloing”), there are certain ways in which tokenization could offer enormous efficiencies. I have been to blockchain conferences on the application of blockchain to property titling, something that is of great and lumbering societal expense.

Big players in the RWA space like Ondo Global Markets and WisdomTree are using Solana. Ondo has tokenized over 200 stocks and ETFs, $450 million in total value, made available to non-US investors. WisdomTree has also done some innovative things in this sector, allowing institutional clients to manage tokenized assets directly on Solana, apply Solana-native applications, and off-ramp to self-custody wallets.

As Solana Foundation’s Nick Ducoff proclaimed:

“As real world assets on Solana surpass $1B, WisdomTree’s decision to expand their full suite of tokenized funds to Solana reflects the demand for expanded access to tokenized RWAs and Solana’s ability to support that demand at scale. This launch also highlights the growing role regulated asset managers play in shaping the future of on-chain finance."

Risks and Opportunities

The risks of Solana should be obvious: it is a novel platform of extreme volatility. Its long-term survival remains unproven. The coin could conceivably drop 75% from here and quite quickly –perhaps this month!

However, there has been a clear sea change in assessment at the federal level as to the future of crypto and blockchain. Solana is being institutionalized –not for its antagonism to fiat currency (a la Bitcoin AKA gold 2.0)—but rather for its prowess as a solution.

The context reminds me of the “Paperwork Crisis of 1967” which was caused by the outmoded manual back-office operational processing of stock orders in the face of massive new trading volumes. During the Go-Go Boom, daily NYSE volume went from 5 to 15 million in 4 years (1964 to 1967). Even clerks working in three shifts seven days a week couldn’t keep up.

The solution to the crisis: computerization. As a platform, Solana might be our modern equivalent, in which case an early investment in a staking ETF like BSOL could be quite valuable in the coming decades.

On Friday, BSOL saw a strong bounce off a possible double bottom (the Feb. 5 and Feb. 12 lows). One day does not a direction make. Being early can also mean being wrong.

However, whether you buy now at $11.28 or wait to see if BSOL drops to into the $2 - $3 range, the ticker and its platform are worth keeping an eye on.

data.title: Toast: A Good House In A Bad Neighborhood
data.description:
Excluding networks like Visa (V) and the card-issuing banks, companies in parts of the payments food chain have taken a severe beating in the last 12 months. Year-to-date declines have also been quite steep. There are two approaches one can take. One is to avoid the sector entirely, which is a fair call. A healthy sector, much like a rising tide, acts like insurance and can paper over mediocre quarterly performances. You get the opposite effect of steeper sells with a weaker industry. The second approach is to find a bottom, as some of these names will likely dive into oversold territories due to sector anxieties. As a part of this second approach, I decided to take a look at Toast (TOST) again.

Q4 Print Was Strong

Toast's Q4 2025 results painted the picture of a company executing brilliantly on operations even as the market demands more. Full-year revenue was up 24% to $6.15 billion with a dramatic but expected change in profitability. GAAP net income hit $342 million in FY25 compared to $19 million a year ago. Free cash flow nearly doubled year-over-year to $608 million, and the company sits on $1.35 billion in cash with no debt.

In terms of the guidance, the adjusted EBITDA range of $775 million–$795 million was a tad softer than expected, and management flagged a 150 basis point hit on the hardware side due to higher memory chip costs in the second half of 2026. I think these are transitory issues rather than structural impairments, and if anything, the earnings proved that the company's unit economics and growth profile remain attractive.

Finally, on valuations, there is a legitimate argument that the stock screens expensive. However, two contextual factors matter.

FY25 was Toast's first year of meaningful GAAP profitability. So the trailing numbers would look optically stretched as the earnings keep scaling. But the growth-adjusted metrics paint a balanced picture. On forward PEG and EV/Sales, the company actually screens well. So while the stock isn't statistically cheap, it isn't pricing in a fragile business model either.

Backdrop of Threats

If you observed the reaction to Toast's earnings, the stock was down ~5% in aftermarket trading but went up ~5% on Friday morning once the inflation data came in better than expected. If I add this macro-based reaction to the ~20% - 30% sector-wide year-to-date decline, I can think of only one thing. The broader market is likely questioning the terminal value of these companies.

On the software side, it is questioning the terminal value of any company that lacks a definitive answer to the Agentic AI threat.

Toast Segments % of 2025 Gross Profits
Subscription services 42.18%
Financial technology solutions 71.94%
Hardware and professional services -13.81%

While the subscription service is 42% of the company's gross profits, I am not sure this specific threat is very relevant to Toast. The agentic AI threat makes sense when a SaaS entity sells based on the number of human users that interact with it. Lower the number of users, the lower the sales figure. Toast's subscription fees are based on the number of devices. In addition, the upsells are based on modules that solve specific business problems. For example, the fees for Kitchen Display Screens are per screen, not per chef. Tools like loyalty or online ordering are monthly additions to the base plan.

Payroll and scheduling are partial exceptions, as they are structured around employee count rather than active user engagement. Over time, this part could face pressure towards flat or bundled pricing, especially if they become table stakes in the broader restaurant operating system. Even so, I'd rank this as a low-intensity risk, closer to a 2 out of 10 in terms of structural threats to Toast.

Notably, a second threat has been gathering steam with bipartisan support for the Credit Card Competition Act. President Trump expressed support for this bill to "stop the out of control Swipe Fee ripoff." Even the National Restaurant Association has a brief supporting this policy.

This measure ranks much higher on the intensity scale in terms of risks to Toast. The Act mandates that large banks (over $100 billion in assets) must have at least two networks for routing transactions, and they cannot both be the two largest networks. By forcing a second option, I think the act intends to set off a price war between the two networks to win the merchant's transaction. This would certainly lower the overall fees Toast manages and profits from and pressure take rates on transactions. There's also a cost component to this development. If you think of private networks, there are a few options like Shazam, NYCE, or Star apart from Discover and American Express (AXP), and supporting them would involve some additional costs in updating the back-end infrastructure. While Toast remains one of the best tickers in this space, it is unlikely to soften the drubbing in the stock if the act does get through.

Great House in a Bad Neighborhood

This is a difficult one to rate and is akin to being torn about a great house in a bad neighborhood. On the one hand, the strong internal execution deserves a "Strong Buy" rating, but the macro threats can get precarious and hint towards a "Sell". I am persisting with a "Buy" still. Why? Because market disconnects of this kind, where operational excellence is overshadowed by sectoral fear, can generate some of the best long-term returns. When the neighborhood does stabilize, premium assets re-rate the quickest. In terms of strategies, I don't think this is a stock to load up on in a single move. Building exposure gradually will give that much needed flexibility if and when volatility intensifies.

data.title: ZIM: Hapag-Lloyd Offer Makes Sense For Everyone, Will It Go Through?
data.description:
I've been covering ZIM Integrated Shipping Services Ltd. (ZIM) during this year. In my last article I upgraded the stock from a sell to a buy due to increased interest in acquiring the company. Since then, MSC denied its interest for bidding for ZIM; however, ZIM confirmed an agreement with Hapag-Lloyd (HPGLY) that will acquire ZIM for $4.2 billion or $35 per share in cash.

Background

ZIM Integrated Shipping Services is an Israeli liner company with a light business model that relies on chartering-in most of its ships. However, just after COVID, in 2021 and 2022, the company was one of the first to order new tonnage, placing orders for 46 newbuilds with deliveries scheduled between 2023 and 2024. This is significant because ZIM ordered those ships at historically low prices, and since then, their value has increased by more than 50%:

While ZIM doesn’t own the ships, they are chartered in under very long term charters and include purchase options as mentioned in the Q3 conference call.

Moreover, for 25 of the 28 LNG vessels, our core strategic capacity, we hold options to extend the charter period as well as purchase options, giving us full control over the destiny of these vessels very much as if we were the vessel owners.

Moreover, liner companies' appetite for new containerships does not seem to have any limits, with new orders reaching new highs despite record orderbook. For those companies will be much more profitable to buy ZIM than continuing ordering newbuilds with delivery scheduled for 2029.

ZIM’s fleet consist of 129 vessels with 60% being newbuilds. Moreover, last year the company ordered ten additional newbuilds with deliveries expected between 2027 and 2028.

Financial Position and Valuation

ZIM has a solid financial position with $3 billion in cash and no traditional debt. The debt that appears in the balance sheet correspond to lease liabilities obligations for the chartered in vessels, which are below market rates so, in reality, those have positive value.

At this point, and given the imminent bid, short term results are irrelevant and what is important is to assess the bid potential. The final bid will be around $4.2 billion, representing a price of $35 per share.

As mentioned in the previous article, I was expecting a bid between $25, since the company rejected Eli Glickman’s revised offer (the initial one was $2.4 billion), and $30, due to certain discount caused by the Israel golden share.

Hapag-Lloyd’s offer exceeds that range and will also address the golden share veto, as FIMI, Israel’s largest private equity fund, will maintain control of the Israel operations.

Just to recap, I valued the 46 initial newbuilds at above $5 billion, while those vessels had a purchase price of around $3.5 billion, meaning that the ships alone have appreciated in value by more than $1.7 billion. Those ships are leased but probably have purchase options at the end of the contracts.

Moreover, the company owns some vessels and containers. Their value could be derived from its balance sheet and is around $1.3 billion, or over $10 per share.

Finally, we must also consider the $3 billion in cash on the balance sheet, or approximately $25 per share.

To resolve the golden share issue, FIMI will acquire ZIM’s owned and Israeli flagged vessels and will maintain the Israeli operations. This way, the golden share shouldn’t be an issue. On the other hand, Hapag-Lloyd will acquire ZIM’s leased vessels and international operations.

Risks

The main risk is that the government vetoes the transaction and the Hapag-Lloyd cannot structure the deal in any other way. However, given the current structure, I believe this will not be an issue. Moreover, ZIM could also sell just the international business or accept Eli’s offer.

If the company can’t secure any deal, I expect it to fall by more than 50%; however, as mentioned before, I expect ZIM to sell some of its assets and pay a generous dividend to reward shareholders.

Another risk is the regulatory approval, but even with the ZIM acquisition, Hapag-Lloyd won't be one of the largest liner operators, which indicates that regulators shouldn’t stop the acquisition.

Conclusion

Hapag-Lloyd’s offer to acquire ZIM seems fair and could satisfy all stakeholders.

Investors should benefit the most as the stock was trading below $15 per share before the last round of rumors and represents more than a 50% premium over Friday’s close.

The Israeli government will continue to control a liner company, and Hapag-Lloyd will benefit from expanding its fleet.

Even if the sale fails, I expect the company to unlock its value by selling some assets and paying a generous dividend using its cash on the balance sheet.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Palantir Obliterated Expectations And The Stock Sold Off: Mispricing At Its Finest
data.description:
Heading into the Q4 print, I was bullish on Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), citing an irrational selloff driven by headlines and the company's high beta with the software industry.

I had a high conviction that the company would surprise the Street's models, which would lead to even more upward sales and earnings revisions for the next quarters.

Well, it seems that I was right with my thesis, as the company obliterated expectations and put out a Q1 guidance for the U.S. commercial segment that was 33% higher than the initial estimates.

That didn't seem to matter, as the irrational pessimism in software dragged the stock down after jumping as much as 11% on the day after earnings.

In this piece, I analyze the highlights in Q4 and some of the key growth drivers behind the blowout quarter and guidance. I advance in this introduction that one of the new offerings, called Chair Reaction, could accelerate the U.S. commercial segment this year, building on top of the AIP platform.

Overall, I remain bullish on Palantir, and I still own the stock, despite seeing some risks later this year if the likes of Anthropic or OpenAI go public. I discuss more below.

Q4 2025 - A Blowout Quarter

Let's start with the top line. Heading into the Q4 print, the Street projected $1.34B in consolidated revenue. That figure included $473.7M from U.S. commercial and $528.4M from U.S. government. As is often the case, Palantir obliterated those expectations. The company reported $1.41B in total revenue, including $507M in U.S. commercial and $570M in U.S. government.

From a guidance perspective, the Street was looking for $1.33B in Q1 and $6.28B for FY26 revenue. The company guided $1.534B in Q1 sales and $7.19B in FY26 revenue. Naturally, this prompted significant upward revisions in the Street revenue models in the days after the results.

The highlight was the guidance for U.S. commercial, which I think was the main driver for the upward revisions in the chart above. The company guided Q1 U.S. commercial revenue of over $3.144B, vs. the Street's $2.36B expectations. That's 33% above expectations!

On the bottom line, the Street was looking for 23 cents in adjusted EPS and $701.1M in adjusted operating income. Well, in line with the tradition, Palantir eviscerated these figures after reporting 25 cents in adjusted EPS and $798.5M in adjusted operating income.

What's Driving The Outperformance?

Without any doubt, the U.S. commercial segment.

This is clearly visible on the chart below, with a dramatic acceleration in growth in this segment since Q1 last year.

In Q4 specifically, the remaining deal value in this segment increased by 145% yoy and 21% sequentially. When RDV is growing faster than revenue, you know the company is in growth mode:

Looking at the company-wide TCV (total contract value), this metric increased by 138% yoy to $4.26B. Once again, above the 70% yoy revenue growth of the company.

From a product development perspective, the company introduced Chair Reaction. In my view, this is the key product to monitor this year. Why?

Just take a look at who this tool is built for:

Exactly, the picks & shovels of AI. Overall, I think Chain Reaction may be one reason the company surprised the Street so much with its Q1 guidance.

Looking at the U.S. government segment, I simply can't be disappointed with the 66% yoy growth reported in Q4 2025. However, what I like about the government segment is its outstanding performance outside of the U.S, with the U.K. being one of the strongest geographical regions:

Fourth quarter international government revenue grew 43% year-over-year and 9% sequentially to $160 million, bolstered primarily by our continued work in the U.K.

In comparison, the non-U.S. commercial segment grew by a microscopic 8% yoy in Q4. Which brings me to the next section.

Risks

The AI mania is mainly concentrated in the U.S. To be honest, I share CEO Alex Karp’s frustration from Q4 2024: Europe’s AI adoption still looks anemic, and it’s frankly disappointing to me.

Therefore, the growth story of the company is still limited to one region: the U.S. In Q4, the U.S. accounted for approximately 77% of total revenue.

So far, it seems that investors are not particularly worried about this concentration. However, in the long run, there is a risk that growth in the U.S. reaches a plateau and, without a tailwind from abroad, the growth story stagnates.

The Street is already projecting a dire slowdown in growth, with a deterioration from 74% in Q1 2026, down to 37% in Q4 2027.

Some growth investors may not love this setup. If Anthropic and OpenAI go public in the next 12–24 months, I wouldn’t be surprised to see some money rotate out of Palantir, chasing other pure-play AI stocks.

For reference, Anthropic projected sales to quadruple to $18B in 2026, with a $55B target in 2027 (3x). On the other hand, OpenAI projects to grow its topline by (2.3x) 130% yoy this year, targeting over $125B in 2029. In other words, the implied CAGR of OpenAI between 2025 and 2029 is about 77% per year.

Finally, there is the beta risk, given the selloff in software in the last few months. Just take a look at the chart below, representing the returns and breadth of the different industries in tech over the last 3 months.

For reference, Palantir falls within Software Infrastructure.

Valuation

Palantir is still an expensive stock, trading at 115x forward earnings. Now, that valuation is lower than what it was a few quarters ago, largely due to the selloff in the software sector and the expansion in the company's forward earnings (the E in the P/E formula).

Conclusion

Do I think valuation is why the stock sold off by over 35% since the highs in October?

Absolutely not.

I think the 35% selloff from the October highs was mainly driven by the irrational pessimism in software due to the "AI will replace software" fallacy. I already discussed my discontent with this narrative in my AppLovin coverage, so I won't get into details here. Overall, I don't think a company/startup/individual is going to successfully vibecode the AIP platform and steal Palantir's existing and potential new customers. It is my view that enterprise AI is a winner-take-all market, and the first few players to win are the ones that will take most of the market share. Regardless of the competition from OpenAI and Anthropic, Palantir is already seen as a clear winner in this space.

From a price target perspective, I think we could see new highs before the end of the year, likely in H2 rather than H1.
